metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's description of the PATH step to match reconcile-bridge.yml exactly: `sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli` (system symlink), not `echo ~/.acli >> $GITHUB_PATH`, so the implementer and the config-as-artifact test both use the canonical form.
findings.finding: The plan's prose description of the 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step says 'append `~/.acli` to `$GITHUB_PATH`', but the authoritative reference (reconcile-bridge.yml line 181) actually uses `sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli` — a system-wide symlink, not a PATH append. The AC mandates mirroring reconcile-bridge 'EXACTLY', so this discrepancy in the plan's description will cause the implementer to produce a different (and potentially non-functional) mechanism.
findings.location: ## What — item 1, 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step description
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'append `~/.acli` to `$GITHUB_PATH`' with 'symlink `$HOME/.acli/acli` into `/usr/local/bin/acli` via `sudo ln -sf`' to match the reconcile-bridge.yml step verbatim.

findings.finding: The plan describes the 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step as 'append `~/.acli` to `$GITHUB_PATH`', but the actual reconcile-bridge.yml step (lines 174-185) does NOT use $GITHUB_PATH at all — it runs `sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli`. The plan claims to reuse these steps 'verbatim in shape', creating a contradiction between the directive ('verbatim in shape') and the description of the mechanism ('append ~/.acli to $GITHUB_PATH'). An implementer following the prose description would produce a step that diverges from the source, and the plan provides no rationale for which variant to use.
findings.location: What §1, bullet 'the `Add acli to PATH and authenticate` step'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'append `~/.acli` to `$GITHUB_PATH`' with 'symlink acli into /usr/local/bin via `sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli`' to match the actual reconcile-bridge.yml step verbatim.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's description of the 'Add acli to PATH' mechanism: reconcile-bridge.yml uses `sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli` (a symlink into /usr/local/bin), NOT `echo ~/.acli >> $GITHUB_PATH`; update both the What section and the AC to reflect the actual step shape.
findings.finding: The plan's description of the 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step says it appends `~/.acli` to `$GITHUB_PATH`, but the actual `reconcile-bridge.yml` step uses `sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli` — not a `$GITHUB_PATH` append at all. The plan claims to 'MIRROR the established reconcile-bridge.yml convention EXACTLY', so the implementation spec is inconsistent with the source it claims to copy.
findings.location: What section, item 1 — 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step description; reconcile-bridge.yml line 181
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'append `~/.acli` to `$GITHUB_PATH`' with 'symlink the binary into /usr/local/bin via `sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli`' to match reconcile-bridge.yml line 181 exactly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step description in the plan to match the actual `reconcile-bridge.yml` mechanism (`sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli`) rather than `echo ~/.acli >> $GITHUB_PATH`, so the implementation and the config-as-artifact AC test both encode the right step.
findings.finding: The plan states the 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step should 'append ~/.acli to $GITHUB_PATH', but the actual reconcile-bridge.yml it mandates mirroring verbatim uses `sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli` (a symlink to /usr/local/bin) — not $GITHUB_PATH appending. An implementor following the plan's prose produces a divergent step; the config-as-artifact AC test asserting 'the acli PATH step' would then encode the wrong mechanism.
findings.location: What section, item 1 — 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step description; reconcile-bridge.yml line 181
findings.suggested_fix: Replace "append `~/.acli` to `$GITHUB_PATH`" in the What section with "symlink the binary to `/usr/local/bin/acli` via `sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli`" to match reconcile-bridge.yml line 181 verbatim.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address whether the `langfuse-trace` job should also run on the weekly schedule or be excluded (e.g. via `if: github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch'` on that job), and document the decision.
findings.finding: The `on.schedule` trigger added to `external-integration.yml` will also fire the existing `langfuse-trace` job (which spins up a Docker-compose Langfuse stack) on every weekly scheduled run. The plan only discusses changes to the `external` job and is silent on the `langfuse-trace` job running on the same schedule, which adds unplanned CI resource consumption and a potential weekly failure vector for the Langfuse stack.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `if: github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch'` to the `langfuse-trace` job so it only fires on manual dispatch, keeping the scheduled run scoped to the Jira/LLM `external` job.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step description in What item 1 to match reconcile-bridge.yml line 181 exactly: `sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli` (symlink into /usr/local/bin), not `echo ~/.acli >> $GITHUB_PATH`; update the config-as-artifact AC to assert the symlink mechanism, not a $GITHUB_PATH append.
findings.finding: The plan instructs implementors to mirror reconcile-bridge.yml's 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step 'verbatim in shape' but then describes the PATH mechanism incorrectly: the plan says 'append ~/.acli to $GITHUB_PATH', while reconcile-bridge.yml line 181 actually uses 'sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli' — a symlink into /usr/local/bin, not a $GITHUB_PATH append. The config-as-artifact test the plan also mandates ('installs acli / puts it on PATH') may be keyed to the wrong mechanism depending on which prose description the implementor follows.
findings.location: What / item 1 — 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate step' description; reconcile-bridge.yml line 181
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'append ~/.acli to $GITHUB_PATH' with 'symlink the binary into /usr/local/bin via `sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli`' in the What item 1 step description. Update the config-as-artifact test AC to assert presence of `sudo ln -sf` (or `ln -sf`) in the PATH step, matching the actual reconcile-bridge.yml source.

findings.finding: The plan misstates how reconcile-bridge.yml puts acli on PATH. The plan says the 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step appends '~/.acli' to '$GITHUB_PATH', but the actual reconcile-bridge.yml step (line 181) uses 'sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli' — a symlink into /usr/local/bin, not a $GITHUB_PATH append. Implementers copying 'the step verbatim in shape' from the plan description will get the wrong implementation; the config-as-artifact test's assertion about 'acli PATH step' may also be keyed to the wrong mechanism.
findings.location: What / Item 1 — 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step description; reconcile-bridge.yml line 181
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'append ~/.acli to $GITHUB_PATH' with 'symlink ~/.acli/acli into /usr/local/bin via sudo ln -sf (matching reconcile-bridge.yml line 181)' in the What section and AC bullet.

findings.finding: The plan's What item 1 describes the `Add acli to PATH` step as 'append `~/.acli` to `$GITHUB_PATH`', but the actual reconcile-bridge.yml convention uses `sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli` — not `$GITHUB_PATH` appending. The plan says to reuse reconcile-bridge's step 'verbatim in shape', creating a contradiction between the description and the cited reference. An implementer following the prose rather than the reference file will produce a non-matching implementation.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'append ~/.acli to $GITHUB_PATH' with 'symlink the binary into /usr/local/bin via `sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli`' in What item 1.

findings.finding: The plan describes the 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step as appending '~/.acli' to '$GITHUB_PATH', but the actual reconcile-bridge.yml it cites as the authoritative source uses 'sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli' (a symlink into /usr/local/bin) — not a $GITHUB_PATH append. This internal contradiction between the plan's prose and the cited prior art means an implementer following the plan text will diverge from reconcile-bridge's actual shape, which also undermines the config-as-artifact test's ability to pin the correct step.
findings.location: What > item 1 (acli install) — 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step description; reconcile-bridge.yml lines 174-185
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'append ~/.acli to $GITHUB_PATH' in the plan with 'symlink the acli binary into /usr/local/bin via sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli', matching reconcile-bridge.yml line 181 verbatim.

findings.finding: The plan's 'What' section describes the 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step as 'append ~/.acli to $GITHUB_PATH', but the actual reconcile-bridge.yml it instructs implementors to mirror 'verbatim in shape' uses `sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli` (a symlink to /usr/local/bin) — never $GITHUB_PATH. An implementor following the plan's prose will produce a step that diverges from the stated authoritative template, and any config-as-artifact test written to assert the $GITHUB_PATH mechanism will test the wrong thing.
findings.location: What section, item 1 — 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step description; reconcile-bridge.yml lines 179-185
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section item 1, replace 'append ~/.acli to $GITHUB_PATH' with 'sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli' to match reconcile-bridge.yml line 181 exactly. In the config-as-artifact test, assert the YAML contains 'ln -sf' or '/usr/local/bin/acli', not $GITHUB_PATH.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the PATH-mechanism discrepancy: the plan says 'append ~/.acli to $GITHUB_PATH' but reconcile-bridge.yml (the stated prior art to mirror 'verbatim in shape') uses `sudo ln -sf ~/.acli/acli /usr/local/bin/acli` — pick one explicitly and update the plan text so the config-as-artifact test can pin the right step shape.
findings.finding: The plan describes the 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step as 'append ~/.acli to $GITHUB_PATH' but the prior art it says to mirror verbatim — reconcile-bridge.yml line 181 — actually uses `sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli`. The two mechanisms are functionally equivalent for shutil.which('acli'), but the plan's description contradicts the reference implementation it claims to copy 'verbatim in shape', leaving an implementor to choose between plan prose and actual prior art.
findings.location: What § item 1 — 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step description; .github/workflows/reconcile-bridge.yml line 181
findings.suggested_fix: Update the plan's step description to read: 'a `sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli` symlink into /usr/local/bin (matching reconcile-bridge.yml line 181)' — dropping the $GITHUB_PATH wording to match the actual prior art.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the exact weekly cron expression in the plan (e.g. `cron: '0 8 * * 1'`) so the config-as-artifact test and the YAML both pin the same agreed-upon value.
findings.finding: The plan says to add a 'weekly' on.schedule cron trigger but never states the actual cron expression (day of week, hour, minute). An executing agent must invent an arbitrary time, risking a mismatch with org-wide scheduling conventions or a noisy/quiet time of day.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to What §1: 'Use `cron: "0 8 * * 1"` (Monday 08:00 UTC) as the schedule expression; the config-as-artifact test must assert this exact string.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact pytest hook primitive (e.g. `pytest_runtest_logreport` accumulating into a `session.stash` counter, finalized in `pytest_sessionfinish`) and clarify whether setup-time fixture skips from `_require_external_opt_in` count toward the 'all-skip' threshold — both are load-bearing for the canary to fire correctly.
findings.finding: The fail-on-all-skip session hook's counter accumulation mechanism is never specified: no hook primitive (pytest_runtest_logreport, pytest_runtest_makereport, pytest_sessionfinish, etc.) is named, no counter storage location is given (module-level mutable, plugin class, session.stash), and the existing _require_external_opt_in autouse fixture calls pytest.skip() at fixture setup time — whether those setup-time skips appear identically in a logreport-based tally vs. sessionfinish session attributes is implementation-defined and load-bearing for whether the canary ever fires. The existing conftest.py has zero counting infrastructure.
findings.suggested_fix: In What item 2, add: 'Implement via `pytest_runtest_logreport`: accumulate `executed` and `skipped` counts into `session.stash[<stash_key>]` for each `report.when == "call"` (executed: `report.passed or report.failed`; skipped: `report.skipped`). Check the totals in `pytest_sessionfinish`. Note: setup-time skips from `_require_external_opt_in` appear as `when="setup", skipped=True` — exclude `when != "call"` from the tally so the hook counts test outcomes, not fixture events.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `timeout-minutes` on the external-integration job (and/or the pytest step) so a hung Jira call surfaces as a fast failure rather than a 6-hour runner stall.
findings.finding: The scheduled workflow specifies no job-level or step-level timeout for the external pytest run. If Jira is slow or unresponsive during test execution, the job can hang until GitHub Actions' 6-hour hard limit, blocking the runner and delaying the failure signal by hours rather than minutes.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `timeout-minutes: 15` at the job level in `external-integration.yml`, and optionally pass `--timeout=60` to pytest (requires pytest-timeout) so individual hanging tests are killed promptly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the plan's spec for the fail-on-all-skip hook being 'a no-op when REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL is unset' is implemented strictly enough to leave `test_external_isolation.py::test_external_dir_all_skipped_without_opt_in` green (it invokes pytest against tests/external/ without REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL and asserts exit code 0).
findings.finding: The new fail-on-all-skip session hook added to `tests/external/conftest.py` is a shared conftest that affects all 12 existing test files under `tests/external/`. These are unenumerated consumers. In particular, `tests/unit/test_external_isolation.py::test_external_dir_all_skipped_without_opt_in` invokes pytest as a subprocess against `tests/external/` with `REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL` scrubbed — if the hook is a true no-op when `REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL` is unset, this existing test is safe; but if there is any implementation subtlety (e.g. hook fires before env check), it could break this existing test.
findings.suggested_fix: Explicitly note in the plan/implementation that the hook must not alter session exit-code or write any pytest failure when REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL is absent, and add this interaction to the behavioral test's assertions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise the fail-on-all-skip hook's threshold to account for always-executing fixture tests: either (a) count only tests marked with `@_skip` / credential-gated tests as the denominator (tracking credential-gated skip ratio rather than raw executed count), or (b) require that at least one *Jira-gated* AND at least one *LLM-gated* test executed, or (c) add an explicit 'at least one test per service tier executed' assertion that is not satisfied by `test_model_requests_enabled` alone.
findings.finding: The fail-on-all-skip canary is structurally defeated by `test_model_requests_enabled.py::test_external_tier_allows_model_requests`, which always executes (never skips) under `REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL=1` regardless of whether any credentials are configured. On a scheduled run missing all Jira and LLM keys, every credential-gated test skips, but this fixture test executes and passes — so `executed_count` is always ≥1, the 'zero executed' threshold never fires, and the run remains green. The canary never turns RED.
findings.suggested_fix: In the conftest hook, rather than checking `executed == 0`, track per-tag or per-marker counts: collect items whose `_skip` skipif would have fired (i.e., credential-gated ones) and fail if ALL of those skipped. Alternatively, add a `pytest.mark.jira_live` marker to Jira-gated tests and assert at least one `jira_live`-marked test executed on a scheduled run.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Harden the 'Download + verify + install acli' step so that a missing or empty ACLI_SHA256 exits with an error (not a warning), enforcing supply-chain integrity on the boundary-crossing path that holds live secrets; do not inherit the soft-check behavior from reconcile-bridge verbatim.
findings.finding: The plan instructs mirroring reconcile-bridge's 'Download + verify + install acli' step verbatim, but reconcile-bridge's own implementation makes SHA256 verification a soft warning (not a hard failure) when ACLI_SHA256 is unset (line 156-159: `if [ -n "${ACLI_SHA256:-}" ]; then sha256sum -c … else echo '::warning::…'`). An operator who provisions ACLI_VERSION but omits ACLI_SHA256 will silently run an unverified third-party binary in the same job that holds JIRA_API_TOKEN and LLM API keys. The plan's AC3 requires ACLI_SHA256 be configured, but enforcement lives only outside the workflow — the workflow itself has no hard gate.
findings.location: What section item 1 ('Download + verify + install acli' step); reconcile-bridge.yml lines 156-159; AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix:
Change the reconcile-bridge-inherited SHA256 block from a conditional warning to a hard gate in the new workflow step:
```bash
if [ -z "${ACLI_SHA256:-}" ]; then
  echo "::error::ACLI_SHA256 repo variable is unset. Set it to the sha256 digest of acli ${ACLI_VERSION}."
  exit 1
fi
echo "${ACLI_SHA256}  $HOME/.acli/acli.tar.gz" | sha256sum -c --strict
```
This closes the supply-chain gap without changing the reconcile-bridge template itself.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare in AC3 (operator-attested config evidence) that the JIRA_API_TOKEN used for the canary must be scoped to the dedicated disposable test project only (project-member role, not org-admin), and document this constraint in the provisioning guidance so operators don't reuse a broader reconcile-bridge token.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that JIRA_API_TOKEN (a real Jira credential) is used for live mutations against a dedicated disposable project, but never declares the required Jira-side permission scope for that token (e.g. project-scoped member vs. org-admin). An over-privileged token (e.g. an org-wide admin API token reused from another integration) would give the scheduled canary — and any binary it executes — write access across the entire Jira org, far beyond the dedicated test project.
findings.location: What section item 1 (Jira env wiring); AC line 3 (operator-attested)

findings.finding: AC item 3 labels JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER as 'secrets' while the What section explicitly designates them as 'repo VARIABLES, not secrets' (sourced via vars.* not secrets.*). An implementer following AC item 3 verbatim would configure them as GitHub secrets and the workflow's `vars.JIRA_URL` / `vars.JIRA_USER` references would silently resolve to empty strings, causing the live Jira tests to keep skipping.
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC item 3 to: 'The JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER repo variables, the JIRA_API_TOKEN secret, and the ACLI_VERSION/ACLI_SHA256 repo variables are configured in the GitHub repo ...' — mirroring the What section's exact distinction.

findings.finding: The plan claims the reused acli install step is 'sha256-checked', but the step in `reconcile-bridge.yml` (lines 156–159) only emits a `::warning::` and continues if `ACLI_SHA256` is unset — it does NOT fail. If `ACLI_SHA256` is not provisioned as a repo variable, the scheduled canary silently installs an unverified binary, undermining the supply-chain integrity the plan intends.
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's What §1, change 'sha256-checked' to 'sha256-checked (ACLI_SHA256 must be set; the step fails if absent)' and add a pre-flight check analogous to the ACLI_VERSION guard at reconcile-bridge.yml lines 150–153 that exits non-zero when ACLI_SHA256 is unset.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC requiring that, after credential provisioning (AC3), a `workflow_dispatch` run of `external-integration.yml` is triggered and the run log confirms ≥1 external test executed (non-zero executed count and no all-skip session failure from the canary hook) — this is the end-to-end proof that the live path actually works.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion exercises the live scheduled execution path end-to-end: the ACs verify YAML structure (config-as-artifact) and hook logic (unit test), but none requires triggering an actual workflow_dispatch or scheduled run with real Jira credentials and confirming ≥1 external test executed (non-zero executed count). A correctly-structured YAML with a broken acli auth or a wrong `_live_jira_ready()` flag would satisfy every AC while the live Jira tests still silently skip — exactly the original problem.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a fifth AC: '[ ] [operator-attested] A workflow_dispatch run of external-integration.yml completes with ≥1 external test executed (canary hook does not fire); screenshot or run URL provided as evidence.' This closes the E6(f) gap without blocking automated CI.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief comment block in `tests/external/conftest.py` (and optionally a CLAUDE.md or ADR entry) explaining the fail-on-all-skip invariant, the REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL gate, and the rationale — so the architectural decision survives past the PR description.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel CI quality-gate pattern (fail-on-all-skip canary hook in conftest + scheduled external run) with no mention of capturing the architectural rationale in CLAUDE.md or an equivalent design doc, leaving future maintainers without context on why the hook exists and when it should or shouldn't fire.
findings.suggested_fix: At the top of the session hook in conftest.py, add a docstring/comment: 'Fail-on-all-skip canary: when REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL is set (scheduled/dispatch runs), a session where every external test skipped means credentials or tooling are missing, producing a false green. This hook turns that into a hard failure. See external-integration.yml for the full context.' Optionally mirror this in CLAUDE.md under a CI quality-gates section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a workflow-level cleanup step with `if: always()` (or a post-job script) that scrubs the dedicated Jira test project of any issues matching a canary-specific label/prefix, providing a retirement path when job cancellation interrupts the `try/finally` block.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no retirement/cleanup path for Jira artifacts created during a scheduled run that is cancelled mid-execution (e.g., via GitHub UI or runner preemption). GitHub Actions job cancellation sends SIGTERM to the runner process, which may interrupt Python's `try/finally` before the delete calls complete, leaving orphaned issues and links in the dedicated test project indefinitely.
findings.suggested_fix: Tag every created issue with a unique label (e.g., `canary-run-<run_id>`) and add a workflow step `if: always()` that queries and deletes any issues with that label, ensuring cleanup even on cancellation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the config-as-artifact test approach: specify whether it reads `external-integration.yml` as raw text (matching `test_ci_workflow_parity.py`'s style) or as parsed YAML, since no prior 'YAML-artifact test' pattern exists in the repo to mirror.
findings.finding: The plan references 'a drift guard mirroring the repo's YAML-artifact tests' as justification for the config-as-artifact test style, implying such a test pattern exists in the codebase. No YAML-loading workflow artifact tests exist anywhere in the test suite — the closest precedent is `test_ci_workflow_parity.py` which reads workflow YAMLs as raw text, not as parsed YAML artifacts. There is no established 'YAML-artifact test' convention to mirror.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief rationale in the plan (or conftest docstring) explaining why the custom sessionfinish hook is chosen over pytest's --fail-on-no-tests-ran flag: specifically that the flag fires on zero-collected (not collected-but-all-skipped) and that the REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL scope guard is required to avoid false failures in non-external runs.
findings.finding: The plan proposes a custom pytest_sessionfinish hook to detect all-skipped external sessions, but does not acknowledge the native pytest prior art: pytest's --fail-on-no-tests-ran CLI flag (available in pytest 7.2+) and the 'pytest-fail-on-no-tests' PyPI package. The custom hook IS the correct mechanism (the flag fires on zero-collected, not all-skipped; and the REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL scope guard is essential), but the plan gives no justification for the custom approach over native options, leaving future maintainers unable to assess whether a simpler built-in solution was considered.

No acceptance criterion exercises the newly wired scheduled workflow end-to-end against real Jira (a real triggered run confirming ≥1 live test executed and the run is green). The config-as-artifact test verifies YAML structure, and the behavioral hook test is offline — neither proves the acli auth step works, the Jira creds reach the test, or the live round-trip executes. The whole motivation of the plan is to detect when the real integration is broken, but no AC proves the new path was ever exercised in practice.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria §1 and §3; What §1 (acli install + auth steps)
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '[operator-attested] After secrets/variables are configured (AC3), manually trigger `external-integration.yml` via `workflow_dispatch` and confirm the run completes with ≥1 external test in PASSED or FAILED state (not all SKIPPED) — attach the run URL as evidence. This proves the acli install, PATH, and Jira auth steps actually work end-to-end.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
